response:
To derive the total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ we need to consider the contribution of each database based on the proportion of queries it handles. The function is the sum of the individual response times for MySQL Oracle and Sybase. Each database's response time is a function of the number of queries it processes. 

The number of queries handled by MySQL is $ \alpha Q $. Substituting this into the MySQL response time function gives $ T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) = a \cdot (\alpha Q)^2 + b \cdot e^{c \cdot \alpha Q} $. 

The number of queries handled by Oracle is $ \beta Q $. Substituting this into the Oracle response time function gives $ T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) = d \cdot \ln(\beta Q + 1) + \frac{e}{\beta Q + 1} $.

The number of queries handled by Sybase is $ (1 - \alpha - \beta)Q $. Substituting this into the Sybase response time function gives $ T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) = f \cdot ((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q)^{\frac{3}{2}} - g \cdot \sqrt{(1 - \alpha - \beta)Q} $.

Adding these three components together we get the total query response time function as $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) = T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) + T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) + T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) $.

To determine the optimal values of $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $ that minimize $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ given the constants $ a = 0.01 $ $ b = 0.005 $ $ c = 0.02 $ $ d = 0.03 $ $ e = 2 $ $ f = 0.04 $ $ g = 0.01 $ and $ Q = 1000 $ we need to compute the response times for different combinations of $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $ ensuring that $ \alpha + \beta \leq 1 $.
